title: Moderate: BGLS hyperbolicity verification is incomplete for the averaging polynomial

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
## Gap Description

In Corollary cor:psd, the polynomial
  f(x, a_1,...,a_n, b_1,...,b_n) = (1/n!) sum_{pi in S_n} prod_{i=1}^n (x - a_i - b_{pi(i)})
is asserted to be "homogeneous and hyperbolic in direction e_1 = (1,0,...,0)"
with only the brief justification "by eq:walsh and Walsh's theorem."

## What Must Be Verified

A polynomial f in R[x_1,...,x_m] is hyperbolic in direction w if:
  (H1) f(w) != 0
  (H2) For all a in R^m, all roots of t |-> f(a + t*w) are real.

For our f and w = e_1:
  t |-> f((x,a,b) + t*e_1) = f(x+t, a, b) = (1/n!) sum_pi prod_i ((x+t) - a_i - b_{pi(i)})

The roots in t are gamma_i(a,b) - x where gamma_i(a,b) are the roots of z |-> f(z, a, b) in z.

(H1): f(e_1) = f(1, 0,...,0) = (1/n!) sum_pi prod_i (1 - 0 - 0) = 1 != 0. [OK]
(H2): The roots gamma_i(a,b) - x are real iff gamma_i(a,b) are real for real a, b.
      Walsh's theorem (Trans. Amer. Math. Soc. 24, 1922) guarantees this.

But Walsh's theorem requires BOTH input polynomials to be real-rooted. Here a and b
can be any real vectors -- they are NOT restricted to be roots of real-rooted polynomials.
Walsh's theorem as proved applies to products of LINEAR factors with real arguments,
which is exactly the form (x - a_i - b_{pi(i)}). So the theorem does apply.

## The Subtle Point

BGLS requires f to be hyperbolic on all of R^m. Walsh's theorem guarantees real roots
of t |-> f(a + t*e_1) for ALL real a (not just a in some cone). This is because
the roots of (1/n!) sum_pi prod_i (z - a_i - b_{pi(i)}) are always real by Walsh
(the argument only requires a_i, b_i in R, not that they form roots of a real-rooted
polynomial). This needs to be stated explicitly with a precise citation to Walsh (1922).

## Why This Matters

If f were not globally hyperbolic, BGLS would only give convexity on the hyperbolicity
cone, not a global PSD Hessian. For the proof to work at all (alpha, beta) with
simple roots of p boxplus_n q, we need the Hessian to be PSD at those specific points
-- which requires f to be hyperbolic in a neighborhood of those points, guaranteed by
the global hyperbolicity (Walsh).
